title: Historic American Buildings Survey

infobox.value: (**HDP**) is a division of the U.S. [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") (NPS). It administers three programs established to document historic places in the United States: Historic American Buildings Survey (HABS), Historic American Engineering Record (HAER), and Historic American Landscapes Survey (HALS). Its records include measured drawings, archival photographs, and written reports, all archived in the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress")' Prints and Photographs Division.

["Historic American Buildings Survey: New Deal Web Guide (Virtual Programs & Services, Library of Congress)"](https://www.loc.gov/rr/program/bib/newdeal/habs.html). *www.loc.gov*. Retrieved 2022-03-31.
["The Historic American Buildings Survey"](https://home.nps.gov/subjects/heritagedocumentation/habs.htm). *National Park Service website - About HABS*. Retrieved 5 Jan 2025.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Historic American Buildings Survey](/w/index.php?title=Historic_American_Buildings_Survey&redirect=no "Historic American Buildings Survey"))

Division of the U.S. National Park Service

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Historic_American_Buildings_Survey_%28HABS%29_Team.jpg/250px-Historic_American_Buildings_Survey_%28HABS%29_Team.jpg)](/wiki/File:Historic_American_Buildings_Survey_(HABS)_Team.jpg)

The Heritage Documentation Programs team measures the [Kentucky School for the Blind](/wiki/Kentucky_School_for_the_Blind "Kentucky School for the Blind") in [Louisville, Kentucky](/wiki/Louisville,_Kentucky "Louisville, Kentucky"), in 1934.

**Heritage Documentation Programs** (**HDP**) is a division of the U.S. [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") (NPS). It administers three programs established to document historic places in the United States: Historic American Buildings Survey (HABS), Historic American Engineering Record (HAER), and Historic American Landscapes Survey (HALS). Its records include measured drawings, archival photographs, and written reports, all archived in the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress")' Prints and Photographs Division.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heritage_Documentation_Programs&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Historic American Buildings Survey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heritage_Documentation_Programs&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Historic American Buildings Survey")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/First_national_bank_US_with_HABS_border.jpg/250px-First_national_bank_US_with_HABS_border.jpg)](/wiki/File:First_national_bank_US_with_HABS_border.jpg)

An HABS photograph of the [First Bank of the United States](/wiki/First_Bank_of_the_United_States "First Bank of the United States") in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia")

In 1933, the Historic American Buildings Survey was established following a proposal by [Charles E. Peterson](/wiki/Charles_E._Peterson "Charles E. Peterson"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) a young [landscape architect](/wiki/Landscape_architect "Landscape architect") in the National Park Service. Peterson proposed that the survey would be "Almost a complete resume of the builder's art."
[[2]](#cite_note-HDP_Website-2)

Though it was founded as a temporary, "ten-weeks" constructive [make-work program](/wiki/Make-work_program "Make-work program") for architects, draftsmen, and photographers left jobless by the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), the Historic American Buildings Survey has endured to this day.

The program was later supported through the [Historic Sites Act](/wiki/Historic_Sites_Act "Historic Sites Act") of 1935.[[3]](#cite_note-OldHouse-3)[[4]](#cite_note-King-4)[[5]](#cite_note-American_Place-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Lindley-6)

Guided by field instructions from [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), the first HABS recorders were tasked with documenting a representative sampling of the nation's [architectural heritage](/wiki/Cultural_heritage "Cultural heritage").[[7]](#cite_note-Lavoie-7)

They began to document the [built environment](/wiki/Built_environment "Built environment") in the United States, carrying out multi-format surveys that has today amassed "more than 581,000 measured drawings, large-format photographs, written histories, and original field notes for more than 43,000 historic structures and sites dating from Pre-Columbian times to the twentieth century."[[3]](#cite_note-OldHouse-3)

By creating an archive of historic architecture, HABS provided a [database](/wiki/Database "Database") of primary source material and documentation for the then-fledgling [historic preservation](/wiki/Historic_preservation "Historic preservation") movement.[[8]](#cite_note-Kolson_Hurley-8) Peterson stated that the survey initially would, "...include public buildings, churches, residences, bridges, forts, barns, mills, shops, rural outbuildings, and any other kind of structure of which there are good specimens extant." The acting Chief of HABS, Catherine Lavoie stated in 2011 that HABS was, "Documenting the worthy and not just the wealthy.”

Earlier private projects that pre-dated HABS included [Eleanor Raymond](/wiki/Eleanor_Raymond "Eleanor Raymond")'s *Early Domestic Architecture of Pennsylvania* (1931), [Charles Morse Stotz](/wiki/Charles_Morse_Stotz "Charles Morse Stotz")'s Western Pennsylvania Architectural Survey, and the *[White Pine Series of Architectural Monographs](/wiki/White_Pine_Series_of_Architectural_Monographs "White Pine Series of Architectural Monographs")*.[[7]](#cite_note-Lavoie-7) Many of their contributors later joined the HABS program.

Notable HABS photographers included [Jack Boucher](/wiki/Jack_Boucher "Jack Boucher"), who worked for the project for over four decades,[[9]](#cite_note-WP-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Malvaney-10)[[11]](#cite_note-MANSHEIM-11)
[Robert W. Tebbs](/wiki/Robert_W._Tebbs "Robert W. Tebbs"),[[12]](#cite_note-12) [Richard Koch](/wiki/Richard_Koch_(architect) "Richard Koch (architect)"),[[13]](#cite_note-SixtyFour-13)[[14]](#cite_note-Levy-14) and [Jet Lowe](/wiki/Jet_Lowe "Jet Lowe").[[15]](#cite_note-Lowe-15)

### Historic American Engineering Record

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heritage_Documentation_Programs&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Historic American Engineering Record")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/RFControlPanel2.jpg/250px-RFControlPanel2.jpg)](/wiki/File:RFControlPanel2.jpg)

An HAER photograph of the [Rocky Flats Plant](/wiki/Rocky_Flats_Plant "Rocky Flats Plant") in [Boulder, Colorado](/wiki/Boulder,_Colorado "Boulder, Colorado")

The Historic American Engineering Record (HAER) program was founded on January 10, 1969, by NPS and the [American Society of Civil Engineers](/wiki/American_Society_of_Civil_Engineers "American Society of Civil Engineers"). HAER documents historic sites, structures, mechanical, and engineering artifacts. The Maritime Administration works with HAER to "document historic vessels prior to their disposal."[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Since the advent of HAER, the combined program is typically called "HABS/HAER". [Eric DeLony](/wiki/Eric_DeLony "Eric DeLony") headed HAER from 1987 to 2003.[[17]](#cite_note-Witcher-17)

### Historic American Landscapes Survey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heritage_Documentation_Programs&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Historic American Landscapes Survey")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/VIEW_OF_CEMETERY_SECTION_NAWS_%28NEW_ADDITION_WEST_SIDE%29_AMONG_THE_EUCALYPTUS_TREES._VIEW_TO_SOUTH._-_San_Francisco_National_Cemetery%2C_1_Lincoln_Boulevard%2C_San_Francisco%2C_San_Francisco_HALS_CA-1-7.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:VIEW_OF_CEMETERY_SECTION_NAWS_(NEW_ADDITION_WEST_SIDE)_AMONG_THE_EUCALYPTUS_TREES._VIEW_TO_SOUTH._-_San_Francisco_National_Cemetery,_1_Lincoln_Boulevard,_San_Francisco,_San_Francisco_HALS_CA-1-7.jpg)

An HALS photograph of [San Francisco National Cemetery](/wiki/San_Francisco_National_Cemetery "San Francisco National Cemetery") in [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco")

In October 2000, NPS and the [American Society of Landscape Architects](/wiki/American_Society_of_Landscape_Architects "American Society of Landscape Architects") established a sister program, the Historic American Landscapes Survey, to systematically document historic American landscapes.[[18]](#cite_note-ASLA-18)

A predecessor, the Historic American Landscape and Garden Project, recorded historic Massachusetts gardens between 1935 and 1940. That project was funded by the [Works Progress Administration](/wiki/Works_Progress_Administration "Works Progress Administration"), but was administered by HABS, which supervised the collection of records.[[19]](#cite_note-Stevens-19)

In 2001, along with the Library of Congress, the NPS, and the American Society of Landscape Architects signed a Memorandum of Understanding which established a working relationship between the three organizations. Following the signing of this agreement, these organizations together signed the Tripartite Agreement in 2010, making "HALS a permanent federal program."[[20]](#cite_note-:0-20)

The NPS deals with the planning and operations of HALS, standardizes the formats and develops the guidelines for recording landscapes.[[20]](#cite_note-:0-20)

### Library of Congress

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heritage_Documentation_Programs&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Library of Congress")]

The permanent collection of HABS/HAER/HALS are housed at the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress"), the national library of the United States. Many images, drawings, and documents are available through the Prints and Photographs Online Catalog, including proposed, demolished, and existing structures; locales, projects, and designs. The HABS and HAER collections are among the largest and most heavily used in the division.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## External links

Wikimedia Commons has media related to ***[Historic American Buildings Survey](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Historic_American_Buildings_Survey "commons:Category:Historic American Buildings Survey")***, ***[Historic American Engineering Record](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Historic_American_Engineering_Record "commons:Category:Historic American Engineering Record")*** and ***[Historic American Landscapes Survey](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Historic_American_Landscapes_Survey "commons:Category:Historic American Landscapes Survey")***.

* [Records of the Historic American Buildings Survey (HABS)/Historic American Engineering Record (HAER) Division](https://www.archives.gov/research/guide-fed-records/groups/515.html) Guide to Federal Records
* [National Park Service (NPS): official Heritage Documentation Programs website](https://www.nps.gov/subjects/heritagedocumentation/index.htm)
* [Library of Congress HABS/HAER/HALS Collection](https://www.loc.gov/collections/historic-american-buildings-landscapes-and-engineering-records)
* [Photographer Stepen Schafer Website: Frequently Asked Questions about HABS, HAER and HALS website](https://habsphotography.wordpress.com/)
* [New England based Photographer Andrew Baugnet specializes in HABS/HAER photographic documentation](https://baug.net/)
* [Historic American Buildings Survey (HABS) Collection](https://hdl.handle.net/1903.1/1445 "hdl:1903.1/1445") at the [University of Maryland Libraries](/wiki/University_of_Maryland_Libraries "University of Maryland Libraries")
